title: Beyond Local Detailed Balance: Microscopic Rates Reshape Nonequilibrium Phase Behavior
authors: Takahiro Kanazawa, Kyogo Kawaguchi, Kyosuke Adachi
abstract: Local detailed balance (LDB) is a central guiding principle for modeling nonequilibrium stochastic dynamics, yet it only constrains the ratio of forward and backward transition rates and does not fix the steady state. Although the functional form of rates under the same LDB has been shown to affect correlation properties in weakly interacting systems, whether it can reshape phase behavior in strongly interacting systems remains unclear. Here, for a two-dimensional driven lattice gas with attractive nearest-neighbor interactions, we consider hopping rates with a parameter that preserves the same LDB but tunes asymmetry along the driving force. We find that this parameter controls qualitative phase behavior: in the homogeneous phase, it reverses the sign of the structure-factor discontinuity and hence the anisotropy in long-range density correlations; in the phase-separated regime, it switches the orientation of anisotropic patterns and their long-time stability. Both effects are coherently captured by an approximate fluctuating hydrodynamic equation. The results demonstrate that, in contrast to equilibrium systems, nonequilibrium phase behavior depends on specific dynamical rules even when following the same LDB.
summary: The paper shows that in nonequilibrium systems, the specific form of microscopic transition rates can control macroscopic phase behavior, such as the orientation and stability of patterns. By tuning a parameter that preserves local detailed balance, the authors demonstrate a switch in the anisotropy of long-range correlations and pattern morphology. This highlights that nonequilibrium phase behavior is more sensitive to dynamical rules than equilibrium systems.
main_problem: Investigating whether the specific functional form of microscopic transition rates can reshape many-body phase behavior and pattern formation in strongly interacting nonequilibrium systems, even when those rates satisfy the same Local Detailed Balance condition.
main_result: The parameter theta, which tunes the asymmetry of hopping rates without violating LDB, can qualitatively change macroscopic phase behavior by reversing the sign of structure-factor discontinuity and switching the orientation and stability of phase-separated patterns.
method: The study uses Monte Carlo simulations, a high-temperature expansion to derive effective interactions, and an approximate fluctuating hydrodynamic equation derived via a path-integral formalism.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional driven lattice gas with hard-core exclusion and nearest-neighbor attractive interactions subject to an external driving field along the x-axis.
key_observables: Structure factor S(k) and its discontinuity at the origin, peak height of the structure factor, cluster analysis (number of connected components), and the autocorrelation function.
important_parameters: theta (asymmetry parameter), U (interaction strength), E (external field strength), and beta (inverse temperature).
main_assumptions: The hydrodynamic derivation relies on a small wave vector expansion up to O(k^2) and assumes additive noise in the linearized regime.
figures_summary: Comparison of MC simulations and hydrodynamic predictions for the structure factor discontinuity, and snapshots showing stable horizontal patterns versus unstable, breaking vertical patterns.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of rate-dependence in LDB systems, describes the parameterized lattice gas model, presents analytical results for two-particle systems, details the hydrodynamic derivation and high-temperature expansion, and concludes with simulation results validating the predicted phase behavior changes.
why_interesting: This is relevant for many-body dynamics as it demonstrates that the specific choice of microscopic dynamical rules can fundamentally alter macroscopic steady-state properties and long-range correlations, even when the thermodynamic driving force is held constant.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Driven lattice gas parameterized by θ and qualitative steady-state phase diagrams. (a) We consider particles with onsite exclusion, nearest-neighbor attraction U, and external field E along the x axis. The parameter θ controls asymmetry in the hopping rate along the field direction, while preserving the same LDB. (b) Phase diagram for homogeneous states with weak attractive interaction (U = 0.5), represented by structure factor discontinuity ∆S . We also plot heatmaps of anisotropic structure factor S (k) at indicated parameter sets. (c) Phase diagram for phase-separated states with strong attractive interaction (U = 2), represented by the peak height H of structure factor, signed...

FIG. 2. θ-dependent configurational biases in the two-particle system with L = 2. (a) Diagonally, horizontally, and vertically aligned con- figurations. (b) Ratio of the steady-state probabilities for vertically and horizontally aligned configurations, Pss v /Pss h [Eq. (4)]. When U = 0 or E = 0, Pss v /Pss h = 1 for any θ.

FIG. 3. θ-dependent anisotropy in long-range density correlation. (a) Discontinuity in the structure factor ∆S as a function of θ for various E, obtained numerically from lattice gas simulations. (b) Counterpart of (a), obtained analytically from fluctuating hydrodynamics.

FIG. 4. θ-dependent morphology and stability of phase separation. (a), (b) Snapshots for typical dynamics at (a) θ = −0.5 (horizontally aligned stable pattern) and (b) θ = 0.5 (vertically aligned unstable pattern). (c) Total number of clusters in the steady state as a function of θ. The brightness suggests the strength of E, and the shadow for each curve indicates the standard deviation. Inset: for E = 1, we plot the autocorrelation of large-scale patterns, Re[Ck(τ)].

FIG. 5. Relaxation dynamics of structure factors with weak attrac- tive interactions. We plot the time evolution of the long-wavelength components of the structure factor S (k) [(kx, ky) = (2π/L, 0) for (a) and (0, 2π/L) for (b)] averaged over independent samples for the pa- rameter sets used in Fig. 1(b). The gray area suggests the time points used for averaging.
